turns.text: I ended things with Alex a month ago.
It was the right call, I know it.
But I feel weirdly empty now.
Their voice is still in my head.
Everything I do gets a silent critique.
Like, is this outfit actually okay?
Or am I just being sloppy again?
I used to love cooking.
Now I just order in.
It feels pointless to make an effort.
Alex always had an opinion on my meals.
Too salty, too plain, too weird.
I'm trying to remember what I liked before them.
Before the constant commentary.
My friends say I seem quieter.
I think I'm just listening for that voice.
Waiting for the correction that isn't coming.
It's exhausting.
I want my own thoughts back.
Where do I even start?
I looked at old photos from before we met.
I was wearing things Alex would've hated.
And I looked genuinely happy.
That hit me hard.
I used to go hiking every weekend.
Alex called it a waste of a day.
So I just… stopped.
I think I need to go back to the trails.
But the thought makes me anxious.
What if I get there and feel nothing?
Or hear their critique in my head?
My friend Sam offered to go with me.
That helped a little.
We're planning for this Saturday.
I'm trying to cook one meal this week.
Something simple, just for me.
No one to judge it.
The quiet in my apartment is still loud.
It's not peaceful quiet yet.
It's the quiet of waiting.
I made pasta last night.
Just garlic and oil.
And I ate it without analyzing it.
That felt like a small win.
But the hike is looming.
Sam texted to confirm.
I almost made an excuse.
Why is this so hard?
It's just a walk in the woods.
But it feels like a test.
A test of who I am now.
Saturday morning came.
I put on my old hiking boots.
They felt stiff but familiar.
Sam drove us to the trailhead.
The first ten minutes were quiet.
I was waiting for that inner critic.
Then I noticed the smell of pine.
And I just… breathed.
I didn't hear Alex's voice at all.
We walked for an hour.
I felt tired in a good way.
Not the drained feeling from before.
Sam asked what I was thinking.
I said I was just noticing things.
The light through the trees.
The sound of my own footsteps.
It was enough.
That night, I looked at the photos we took.
My smile looked real again.
Not forced.
I want to hold onto that feeling.
But I'm scared it'll fade.
The quiet in my apartment felt different tonight.
Less like waiting.
More like… mine.
I think I'll cook again tomorrow.
Maybe try that old recipe I loved.
The one Alex always rolled their eyes at.
I'm starting to remember what I like.
I made that recipe tonight.
It was messy and a little spicy.
And I loved every bite.
No one was there to roll their eyes.
I ate it at the counter, standing up.
Just because I wanted to.
The quiet felt comfortable.
Like my own company is enough.
I'm planning another hike next weekend.
Maybe I'll go alone this time.
To see how it feels.
I don't need a test anymore.
I'm just going.
That voice in my head is getting quieter.
It's not gone.
But my own thoughts are louder now.
I'm remembering who I was.
And starting to like who I am.
This feels like enough for now.
Thanks for listening.
I quit my job last week.
Just walked out of the corner office for good.
Ten years of my life, gone just like that.
I thought I’d feel free.
But I just feel empty.
I’m sitting here staring at the wall.
My phone is so quiet now.
No more frantic emails at 2 a.m.
No more impossible quarterly targets.
My wife says I should rest.
But I don’t know how to rest.
My identity was that job.
Who am I without it?
I keep checking my old work email out of habit.
It’s just… silence.
I miss the adrenaline sometimes.
But I hated the constant dread more.
My body still feels tense, like I’m waiting for a crisis.
Maybe I need to figure out what I actually want.
Not what the company wanted from me.
I used to dream about traveling with Sarah.
We never had the time.
Now I have all the time in the world.
And I'm too paralyzed to plan anything.
What if I just wasted my best years?
I sacrificed so much for that title.
Missed birthdays, anniversaries.
Sarah was so patient.
But I saw the disappointment in her eyes.
I promised her things would change.
Now I'm afraid I don't know how.
Maybe I'm just afraid of being ordinary.
That job made me feel important.
Without it, I feel invisible.
I keep thinking about my dad.
He worked himself into the ground too.
He never got to enjoy his retirement.
I don't want that to be me.
But the ambition is still there, like a ghost.
It's a hard habit to break.
I looked at our wedding album yesterday.
We looked so hopeful.
I want to get back to that version of me.
But where do I even start?
Sarah suggested I just take a walk today.
It felt strange not having a destination.
I kept checking my watch out of habit.
Old patterns die hard, I guess.
Maybe I need a project, but a small one.
Something just for me, not for a resume.
I used to love building model ships.
Haven't touched them in years.
The box is still in the garage.
Maybe I'll open it tomorrow.
It feels silly, honestly.
Like it's not a real use of time.
That's the old voice talking.
I have to quiet it.
Sarah asked me about dinner plans for next week.
For the first time, I could actually say yes.
I said yes to dinner with Sarah next week.
It felt like a real decision.
Maybe small choices are the way forward.
I opened the model ship box today.
The smell of the wood brought it all back.
I just sat with the pieces for a while.
No plan, no deadline.
It was… peaceful.
I think I'll work on it a little each day.
Sarah smiled when she saw it on the table.
That meant more than any bonus.
I'm still scared of the big 'what next'.
But maybe I don't need the answer yet.
I deleted the work email app from my phone.
That was harder than I thought.
But it felt like a necessary cut.
I'm starting to sleep through the night.
My body is finally unwinding.
I looked at a travel site yesterday.
Just browsing, no pressure.
I booked a weekend trip for us.
Just a cabin by the lake.
Sarah cried when I told her.
Happy tears, I think.
The ship's hull is taking shape.
I'm enjoying the slow process.
I don't miss the frantic pace.
Not even a little.
I'm starting to see a different future.
One built on small, real things.
Maybe I'll mentor part-time someday.
But on my own terms.
For now, the quiet is enough.
I feel like I'm finally present.
Sarah says I look lighter.
I feel lighter.
That corner office feels like someone else's life.
I'm okay with that.
I think this is what healing feels like.
One small step at a time.
I feel like a total fraud in all my classes.
Everyone else seems to get it instantly.
I’m just pretending to follow along.
My anxiety is getting worse every week.
I’m terrified it’ll make me fail.
Like in my calculus class yesterday.
The professor called on me randomly.
My mind just went completely blank.
I could feel my face getting hot.
I mumbled something and he moved on.
But I sat there shaking for ten minutes.
It’s not just that one class, either.
My English seminar discussion is tomorrow.
I’ve read the material three times.
But I’m convinced I have nothing smart to say.
I keep thinking they’ll figure me out.
That they’ll see I don’t belong here.
My roommate seems so confident and prepared.
It just makes me feel more alone.
I need to figure out why this is happening.
Maybe it started in high school.
I was always the quiet one.
I got good grades, but it felt like luck.
My parents were so proud when I got in here.
Now I’m scared I’ll disappoint them.
I haven’t told anyone how bad it is.
Not even my roommate.
I just pretend everything’s fine.
But the pretending is exhausting.
I skipped my first calc homework last week.
I was too scared to even start it.
That’s never happened before.
I’m falling behind already.
And the English seminar is in twelve hours.
I keep rereading my notes.
But they just look like nonsense.
What if I actually don’t belong here?
What if they made a mistake admitting me?
I feel like I’m waiting to be exposed.
I don’t know how to make it stop.
Maybe I should try talking to someone.
But who would even understand?
The campus counseling center, maybe.
I looked it up once.
But I never booked an appointment.
What would I even say?
Back to the seminar.
I decided to write one question down.
Just one, to have in my hand.
It’s about a character’s motivation.
Maybe that’s enough for tomorrow.
It feels like a tiny step.
But the calc homework is still there.
I missed two assignments now.
I need to email the professor.
Admit I’m struggling.
That’s terrifying, though.
What if he thinks I’m lazy?
Or not cut out for it?
I don’t know which is worse.
But I have to try something.
I drafted an email to my calc professor.
Just said I’m struggling with the material.
I haven’t sent it yet.
The seminar is in a few hours.
I’m holding onto my one question.
Maybe I can just ask it.
Even if my voice shakes.
After class, I’ll send the email.
That’s the plan.
I did it.
I asked my question in the seminar.
The professor actually said it was insightful.
I felt my shoulders relax a little.
Then I sent the email to my calc professor.
He replied already.
He offered to meet and go over the assignments.
He didn’t call me lazy.
I feel a weird mix of relief and dread.
But I booked the meeting for Friday.
I'm trying to hold onto that small win.
The seminar didn't expose me.
Maybe I can build on that.
But the dread about Friday is still there.
Going over missed work feels like a test.
What if I still don't get it?
I need to prepare for that meeting.
Just try the first problem tonight.
One problem, that's all.
I think I'll look up the counseling center again.
Maybe I can book an appointment next week.
Just to talk about the anxiety.
Not telling my parents yet, though.
I need to figure this out for myself first.
The pretending is still exhausting.
But maybe it doesn't have to be total.
I asked for help with calc.
That's not nothing.
I'm going to try that first problem now.
And maybe breathe a little easier tonight.
I sent my daughter a letter last month.
Haven't heard a word back.
It’s been five years since we last spoke.
I said some awful things during that fight.
I was in a bad place back then.
But that’s no excuse.
I’ve been sober for three years now.
I wanted her to know that.
The silence is just… heavy.
It feels like a final judgment.
Maybe she’s just not ready.
Or maybe she’ll never be.
I keep checking my phone.
Every notification makes my heart jump.
But it’s never her.
I don’t know what else to do.
Apologizing felt like the right first step.
Now I’m just stuck in this waiting.
It’s bringing up a lot of old guilt.
I guess I need to sit with that.
I keep rereading the letter I sent.
Wondering if I said enough.
Or maybe I said too much.
I told her I’m in therapy now.
That I understand why she left.
I didn’t ask for forgiveness.
Just a chance to talk.
Her birthday is next month.
She’ll be twenty-eight.
I missed so many.
Part of me wants to send a card.
But that might feel like pressure.
I don’t want to mess this up.
My sponsor says to focus on my own work.
That I can’t control her response.
It’s just so hard to accept.
The fight was about her moving away.
I accused her of abandoning me.
God, that was cruel.
I was terrified of being alone.
I think that fear is still there.
Just quieter now.
My therapist asked what I'm really hoping for.
I said just to know she's okay.
But that's not the whole truth.
I want her back in my life.
Even a little bit.
I found an old picture of us today.
She was about ten.
We looked happy.
It's hard to reconcile those memories.
With what I became later.
Maybe that's who she's protecting.
That little girl.
From me.
I won't send the birthday card.
It feels like respecting her silence.
Even if it hurts.
I'll write it anyway.
Just not send it.
Writing the card helped a little.
It felt like talking to her.
I put it in my desk drawer.
Maybe I'll read it again on her birthday.
My sponsor asked how I'm handling the wait.
I told him it's a daily practice.
Letting go of the outcome.
But I still hope.
Therapy this week was about boundaries.
Hers and mine.
I see now my letter crossed one.
I reached out for my own peace.
Not necessarily for hers.
That's a hard truth.
So my work is to sit with it.
To truly accept her choice.
Even if it's silence.
I think I can do that.
It just takes time.
Like everything else.
I’m going to leave the door open.
But I won’t stand in the doorway.
That’s the difference now.
Her birthday came and went.
I read the card I wrote.
It was enough.
I realized something.
My amends aren’t about her reply.
They’re about changing my own patterns.
And I have.
The silence still aches sometimes.
But it’s not a verdict anymore.
It’s just her reality.
And I can live with that.
I’ll keep working my program.
Keep showing up for my own life.
That’s the real bridge.
The one I’m building to myself.
Thanks for listening.
I think I’m done for now.
I just got diagnosed with lupus.
The doctor mentioned a lot of treatment options.
I'm feeling pretty overwhelmed by it all.
Can you help me break this down?
She started me on hydroxychloroquine.
That's Plaquenil, right?
I'm supposed to take it daily.
But what about side effects?
She also mentioned steroids if flares get bad.
Prednisone, I think.
I'm worried about weight gain from that.
And there were other drugs too.
Like methotrexate or biologics.
How do I even choose?
My main symptoms are joint pain and fatigue.
They're pretty constant right now.
I just want to feel functional again.
Let's start with the hydroxychloroquine.
What should I expect from it?
And how long until it works?
She said it could take months to really work.
That feels like forever.
I have an eye exam scheduled, too.
For the Plaquenil retinal risk.
What about the methotrexate option?
My doctor said it's stronger.
But it involves weekly pills or injections.
And I'd need regular blood tests.
That sounds intense.
My fatigue is so bad I can barely work.
I'm a teacher, so I'm on my feet all day.
Missing work isn't an option right now.
Maybe I should push for a rheumatologist referral.
My GP diagnosed me, but she's not a specialist.
I had a positive ANA test, by the way.
And my sed rate was elevated.
Does that mean my lupus is more active?
I also get this weird butterfly rash sometimes.
It comes and goes.
I just want to know the best next step.
I think I will ask for that rheumatologist referral.
But in the meantime, I'm starting the Plaquenil.
Should I track my symptoms daily?
Maybe a journal would help.
The joint pain is worse in the mornings.
And the fatigue hits around 2 PM.
I'm also wondering about diet changes.
I've heard some people avoid gluten or nightshades.
Is that evidence-based or just anecdotal?
Back to the meds, though.
If the Plaquenil doesn't help in a few months, what then?
Would adding methotrexate be the next move?
The blood test monitoring worries me a little.
But if it lets me keep teaching, it's worth it.
I had another question about the ANA.
My titer was 1:320, homogeneous pattern.
Does the pattern matter for treatment?
I'm trying to learn all this new language.
It's a lot to absorb.
Okay, so my plan is to start the journal and get the specialist referral.
I called my GP's office for the referral today.
They said it could take a few weeks.
I started the symptom journal, though.
Already noticing the morning stiffness is worse on rainy days.
Is that a common lupus thing?
Back to the diet question.
I might try cutting out gluten for a couple weeks.
Just to see if it affects my fatigue.
But I won't stop my meds, obviously.
Had my first eye exam for the Plaquenil.
Everything looked good, which is a relief.
I'm two weeks into the hydroxychloroquine now.
No real change yet, but I know to be patient.
The fatigue is still crushing in the afternoons.
I had to take a half-day last week.
That's what makes me think about stronger treatment.
Maybe I'll bring up methotrexate with the rheumatologist right away.
If the blood tests aren't too bad, I could manage.
My main goal is to get through the school year.
So I need a plan that works faster.
I got the rheumatologist appointment.
It's in three weeks.
I'm going to ask about methotrexate then.
The journal is showing the afternoon fatigue is my biggest issue.
The gluten-free trial didn't really help.
So back to focusing on medication.
One month on Plaquenil now.
Still waiting for it to kick in.
I'm accepting that I might need a combo approach.
Maybe Plaquenil plus a low-dose steroid short-term.
Just to bridge me until the stronger drug works.
I'll ask the specialist about that strategy.
I feel less overwhelmed now.
Having a concrete plan helps.
I'll track symptoms until the appointment.
And I'll keep up with the eye checks.
Thanks for helping me sort through this.
I think I know what to discuss next.
I'm ready for that appointment.
I'll focus on getting through the next few weeks.
I'm so overwhelmed with Dad's meds.
He's on twelve different daily prescriptions.
And I'm coordinating four specialists for him.
Last month, two of his drugs interacted badly.
It sent him to the ER with dizziness.
I'm terrified it'll happen again.
His cardiologist just added a new blood thinner.
But his neurologist prescribed an NSAID last week.
I think those can conflict, right?
I have a spreadsheet, but it's a mess.
His primary care doctor doesn't see the full picture.
None of the specialists talk to each other.
I'm the only one with the complete list.
He takes pills at 8 AM, noon, 6 PM, and bedtime.
Some are with food, some on an empty stomach.
I set alarms on my phone for all of them.
But I missed one last Tuesday when I was at work.
He got confused and took a double dose.
Thankfully, that time it was okay.
I need a better system to catch these interactions before they happen.
The new blood thinner is Eliquis.
The NSAID is naproxen.
I looked them up together.
It says risk of serious bleeding.
I called his neurologist's office this morning.
They said they weren't aware of the Eliquis.
So they just refilled the naproxen.
I feel like I'm the only safety net.
His kidney function isn't great either.
That's from his last labs in March.
Both those drugs can affect kidneys.
I'm trying to get a pharmacist review.
Our local pharmacy said they'd need all twelve meds.
I'm compiling that list for them now.
But what about his over-the-counter stuff?
He takes aspirin sometimes for aches.
And a daily multivitamin with vitamin K.
I heard vitamin K can interfere with blood thinners.
This is getting even more complicated.
I need to talk to his cardiologist about the naproxen.
I left a message for his cardiologist.
About the naproxen and Eliquis.
The pharmacy just called back.
They flagged three potential interactions.
Including the naproxen, like I thought.
They also mentioned his metformin and kidneys.
I need to update my spreadsheet with their notes.
But I'm wondering about a medication manager.
Like one of those weekly pill organizers.
A fancy one with alarms might help.
Though he's resistant to new gadgets.
His cardiologist's nurse just returned my call.
She said to stop the naproxen immediately.
And to use Tylenol for pain instead.
That's a relief, but also frustrating.
Why wasn't this caught earlier?
Now I have to tell his neurologist.
And update all the medication lists.
I'm also worried about cost.
Some of these organizers are expensive.
I found a basic weekly organizer online.
It's under twenty dollars.
I think I'll start with that.
I told his neurologist about stopping the naproxen.
They agreed with the cardiologist.
So that's one interaction resolved.
But the pharmacy flagged two others.
One is his blood pressure med and an OTC decongestant.
He takes that in the winter.
I'll add all OTCs to my list.
The other is his statin and grapefruit juice.
He loves grapefruit juice.
I'll have to talk to him about that.
Maybe I can schedule a medication review with his primary.
With the updated list from the pharmacy.
That might get everyone on the same page.
The organizer arrives tomorrow.
I'll fill it together with him.
Hopefully it reduces the daily alarm chaos.
One step at a time, I guess.
The pill organizer arrived.
We filled it together this evening.
He seemed okay with it.
I scheduled the primary care review.
It's for next Tuesday.
I'll bring the pharmacy's flagged list.
I talked to him about the grapefruit juice.
He agreed to switch to orange juice.
That's a win.
I updated the spreadsheet with everything.
Including the OTC decongestant warning.
My phone alarms are down to just four now.
One for each time slot.
That feels much more manageable.
I'm still the central hub, though.
But at least the system is tighter.
No more double doses, I hope.
I feel less terrified today.
Just vigilant.
Thanks for listening to all this.
I've been tracking my meals and energy crashes for months.
My blood sugar seems to plummet a few hours after eating.
It's been happening more often lately.
I get shaky, sweaty, and really foggy.
I'm trying to figure out what triggers it.
My doctor mentioned reactive hypoglycemia as a possibility.
But we haven't done any formal testing yet.
I started a food and symptom log back in January.
I write down everything I eat and the time.
Then I note any crashes, usually mid-afternoon or late morning.
It's frustrating because I eat what I think are healthy meals.
Like oatmeal with fruit for breakfast.
But two hours later, I'm crashing hard.
My husband thinks I should see an endocrinologist.
I'm just not sure where to start.
Looking at my log, the pattern isn't obvious yet.
Maybe it's the type of carbs I'm eating.
Or maybe I need to pair them with more protein.
I want to understand this better before my next doctor's visit.
Can you help me spot any patterns in my symptoms?
I just had a crash after lunch today.
I ate a turkey sandwich on whole wheat.
That should have been balanced, right?
Maybe the bread is the issue.
My log shows crashes after pasta dinners too.
Even with chicken and vegetables.
It's so inconsistent though.
Sometimes I'm fine after similar meals.
My husband scheduled an endocrinologist consult for next month.
It's on April 15th.
I want to bring solid data to that appointment.
Should I start checking my blood sugar at home?
I bought a glucose monitor last week.
But I'm nervous to start poking myself.
The readings might show what's really happening.
My fasting number this morning was 92.
That seems normal, from what I've read.
But two hours after breakfast, I felt awful.
I didn't test then, which was a mistake.
I'll test next time I feel a crash coming on.
I tested after lunch today when I felt shaky.
My reading was 68.
That seems low, right?
It confirms something is dropping.
But why after a balanced meal?
Maybe my insulin spikes too high.
I read about that with reactive hypoglycemia.
I'm going to try smaller, more frequent meals.
Starting tomorrow.
I'll add more fat and protein to each snack.
Like nuts or cheese.
My husband is being really supportive.
He's helping me prep these meals.
I still feel anxious about the endocrinologist.
What if they don't find anything?
At least I'll have these glucose logs.
I tested again after an afternoon snack.
It was 74, and I felt okay.
So maybe the frequent eating helps.
I'll keep this up until the appointment.
The smaller meals are helping a bit.
But I still had a crash yesterday.
My reading was 65 after a big salad.
Maybe the dressing had hidden sugar.
I'm reading labels more carefully now.
My husband found a dietitian who specializes in this.
We booked a session for next week.
I want to go in with good questions.
What should I ask about meal timing?
Or about getting a continuous glucose monitor?
The endocrinologist appointment is coming up fast.
I've compiled two months of logs.
It shows my crashes clearly.
The numbers don't lie.
I'm feeling less anxious now.
Having a plan helps.
The dietitian might have more immediate strategies.
I'll bring my logs to her too.
Maybe we can pinpoint the worst triggers.
I'm ready to figure this out.
The dietitian appointment was helpful.
She thinks it's definitely reactive hypoglycemia.
Her advice was similar to what I've been trying.
But she emphasized fiber even more.
I'm adding beans and lentils to meals.
My energy has been more stable this week.
I had my endocrinologist appointment yesterday.
She reviewed all my logs and glucose numbers.
She agreed with the reactive hypoglycemia diagnosis.
She ordered an oral glucose tolerance test to confirm.
That's scheduled for next Friday.
I'm not looking forward to it.
But it will give us a clear picture.
She also approved a trial of a continuous glucose monitor.
That feels like a big step forward.
I'm glad I gathered all that data.
It made the appointment so productive.
I feel like I'm finally on the right path.
Thanks for helping me sort through this.
I'll focus on the test and the CGM now.
I'm scheduled for a total knee replacement next month.
I want to go over the pain management plan first.
Can you outline what's typically used?
I'm especially worried about opioid risks.
My surgeon mentioned a nerve block.
How does that work alongside other meds?
And what about after I go home?
I have a history of nausea with strong painkillers.
That's a big concern for me.
Are there non-opioid options they might use?
I've heard about something called a multimodal approach.
Is that common for knee replacements?
Also, how long is the heavy medication phase usually?
My goal is to manage pain but stay clear-headed.
I need to be able to do the physical therapy.
What are the biggest risks with the nerve block?
And the risks of long-term opioid use post-surgery?
I'm trying to weigh all this before I consent.
Let's start with the hospital stay plan.
What should I expect right after surgery?
Will I be on an IV pain pump right away?
And will they give anti-nausea meds proactively?
I had a bad reaction to morphine once.
That's in my records, but I want to double-check.
How effective is the nerve block usually?
Does it wear off suddenly?
My friend said her pain spiked when hers did.
What's the plan to prevent that?
Also, I'm 62 and take a blood thinner.
Does that affect the nerve block safety?
My surgeon said we'd pause it before surgery.
But I'm not sure about the timing.
Let's talk about the home plan next.
How many days of opioids are typically prescribed?
I really want to minimize that.
Can I use Tylenol and ibuprofen instead early on?
I've read about gabapentin sometimes being used.
Is that an option for me?
What about ice machines or compression devices?
I'm willing to try anything non-medication first.
So the nerve block should cover the first day or two?
What if the pain breaks through before it wears off?
I want a clear transition plan.
For home, maybe a short opioid course is unavoidable.
But I'd like a strict limit, like five days' worth.
Can we plan to switch to Tylenol and ibuprofen after that?
I'm okay with gabapentin if it helps.
My main fear is dependency.
Also, the nausea.
They'll give me anti-nausea meds to take home, right?
What about the ice machine?
Is that more effective than just bags of ice?
I'm willing to rent one.
Let's talk risks again.
For the nerve block, is nerve damage a real possibility?
Even with the blood thinner pause?
And constipation from opioids is almost guaranteed.
I should start a stool softener immediately.
This is helping me feel more prepared.
I think I can consent with these clarifications.
I'll ask for a five-day opioid limit at my pre-op appointment.
And confirm the anti-nausea meds are on my discharge list.
The ice machine rental seems worth it.
I'll start the stool softener the day I get home.
What's the typical gabapentin dose after surgery?
And how long is it usually taken?
I'm feeling better about the plan.
But I still have one big worry.
What if the pain is worse than expected?
Is there a backup plan if my limits aren't enough?
Maybe we can schedule a check-in call a few days after.
That would ease my mind.
I'll also confirm the blood thinner pause timing.
Right, I need to ask about that specifically.
Okay, I think I'm ready to consent.
I'll sign with these understandings.
Thanks for walking through it all.
